text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
ONE-YEAR POST-ALLOGENEIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT SURVEILLANCE

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Marcus T. Delgado, 49-year-old male. History of de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M1, with FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio and DNMT3A R882H mutation, ELN 2022 adverse risk. Treated with 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine/daunorubicin) plus midostaurin achieving complete morphologic and MRD-negative remission. Subsequently underwent myeloablative conditioning (fludarabine/busulfan, FluBu4) followed by matched unrelated donor (10/10 HLA-matched) allogeneic peripheral blood stem cell transplant. Course complicated by mild chronic graft-versus-host disease involving skin and oral mucosa, now resolved off immunosuppression. History of prior mild hepatosplenomegaly at AML diagnosis, normalized on post-induction imaging. This examination is performed as routine one-year post-transplant disease surveillance.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast performed at approximately day +90 post-transplant.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with administration of 100 mL of iohexol IV contrast (iodine concentration 300 mg/mL) in the portal venous phase following standard power injection at 2.5 mL/sec. Axial images acquired at 1.25 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reformations in coronal and sagittal planes. No oral contrast administered. No significant motion or beam hardening artifact. Radiation dose within ALARA guidelines (CTDIvol 8.4 mGy, DLP 420 mGy-cm).

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well aerated bilaterally. No focal area of consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or airspace disease is identified. There are no discrete pulmonary nodules in either lung. No endobronchial lesion. The central airways are patent and unremarkable to the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally. The lung parenchyma appears homogeneous without evidence of interstitial thickening, air trapping, or mosaic attenuation pattern to suggest obliterative bronchiolitis. No honeycombing. No pleural effusion on either side. No pneumothorax.

Heart and Pericardium: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. No pericardial effusion. The visualized great vessels, including the thoracic aorta, main pulmonary artery, and superior vena cava, are normal in caliber without filling defect or aneurysmal dilatation. Coronary artery calcification: none identified.

Mediastinum and Hila: No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal lymphadenopathy. The pretracheal, precarinal, paratracheal, aortopulmonary window, and subcarinal nodal stations are all within normal limits in size, with no individual node exceeding 7 mm in short-axis diameter. The hilar structures are symmetric. No hilar adenopathy or hilar mass. The thymus is not visualized, as is expected in an adult patient with prior myeloablative conditioning and immune reconstitution.

Axilla: No axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally. The largest axillary lymph node seen is a 6 mm ovoid node on the right, which is flat and fatty-hilum-preserving, well within normal limits.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No chest wall mass or soft tissue abnormality. No rib lesions. No skin thickening.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size, with a craniocaudal span of approximately 13.9 cm, essentially unchanged from the prior study. The hepatic parenchyma enhances uniformly and homogeneously in the portal venous phase. No focal hepatic lesion is identified. No hypo- or hypervascular mass. The liver contour is smooth. No capsular retraction. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is present with a thin, non-thickened wall. No gallstones identified. The common bile duct measures 4 mm, within normal limits.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size, measuring approximately 8.8 cm in longest craniocaudal dimension, slightly decreased compared to the 9.1 cm measured at the day +90 scan and markedly diminished from the 14.1 cm noted at initial AML diagnosis. The splenic parenchyma enhances in a homogeneous lobular pattern characteristic of normal splenic tissue. No focal splenic lesion. No splenic infarct. No subcapsular collection.

Pancreas: The pancreas is visualized in its entirety and is normal in size, morphology, and enhancement pattern. Head, body, and tail are unremarkable. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding or fluid collection.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and configuration. No adrenal nodule or mass. No limb thickening.

Kidneys and Ureters: Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and position. The renal parenchyma enhances symmetrically and uniformly. No focal renal mass or cyst. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding. The ureters are not dilated to the extent visualized. This appearance is stable from prior study. Note is made of prior mildly elevated creatinine in the early post-transplant period attributed to tacrolimus nephrotoxicity; no morphologic sequelae of this are identified.

Stomach and Small Bowel: The stomach is partially distended with appropriate wall thickness. The visualized loops of small bowel are of normal caliber and wall thickness without evidence of bowel wall thickening, stranding, or fold pattern abnormality. No dilated loops to suggest obstruction.

Colon: The large bowel is not distended. No colonic wall thickening or pericolic stranding identified. No colonic lesion.

Aorta and Major Vessels: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber throughout with smooth walls and no evidence of aneurysmal change. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and bilateral renal arteries appear widely patent. The inferior vena cava and portal vein are patent without filling defect or thrombosis.

Lymph Nodes (Abdomen): No enlarged retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or portocaval lymphadenopathy is identified. Several small retroperitoneal nodes measuring up to 6 mm in short axis are seen and are within normal limits. No pathologic nodal clusters.

Peritoneum and Ascites: No free intraperitoneal fluid. The peritoneal surfaces appear smooth. No evidence of peritoneal soft tissue infiltration or omental thickening.

PELVIS:

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially filled and has a smooth wall without focal thickening or intraluminal mass.

Prostate: The prostate gland is normal in size for patient age without discrete nodule.

Rectum and Sigmoid: No rectal or sigmoid wall thickening. No perirectal soft tissue abnormality.

Iliac Vessels: The bilateral common iliac, external iliac, and internal iliac arteries and veins are patent. No filling defect or thrombosis identified.

Lymph Nodes (Pelvis): No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Bilateral inguinal regions demonstrate small flat lymph nodes with fatty hila, within normal limits.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:

The visualized bony structures are thoroughly evaluated. The spine, pelvis, ribs, and proximal femora are assessed. No discrete lytic or blastic osseous lesion is identified. The vertebral bodies are normal in height and alignment. No pathologic compression fracture. The bone density appears grossly preserved on CT attenuation, though formal densitometry is not assessed on this examination. The bone marrow attenuation is homogeneous throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton without focal areas of abnormal low attenuation (which would be expected with marrow infiltration by leukemia), patchy sclerosis, or permeative destructive change. The marrow signal characteristics are diffusely homogeneous, in keeping with the reconstituted donor marrow seen on serial bone marrow biopsy studies. No periosteal reaction. No cortical destruction.

Soft Tissues (Extraperitoneal): No soft tissue mass in the extraperitoneal retroperitoneum, pelvic sidewalls, or subcutaneous tissues. No evidence of extramedullary hematopoietic tissue or soft tissue chloroma. The musculature appears symmetric and without focal lesion.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of AML recurrence. No discrete lymphadenopathy in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No solid organ mass or focal hepatic lesion. No extramedullary leukemic involvement (no chloroma or granulocytic sarcoma).

2. Spleen and liver are both normal in size, with the spleen measuring 8.8 cm, continuing the trend toward normalization first established on the day +30 post-induction response assessment CT, when prior leukemia-related hepatosplenomegaly fully resolved.

3. The lungs are clear without consolidation, nodule, or interstitial change. Specifically, no features to suggest pulmonary chronic GVHD (bronchiolitis obliterans pattern) are identified; the lung parenchyma is homogeneous without mosaic attenuation or air trapping on these portal venous phase images.

4. No osseous lesion. Diffusely homogeneous marrow attenuation throughout the axial skeleton is consistent with engrafted and reconstituted marrow, without imaging findings to suggest leukemic marrow infiltration.

5. Overall, this one-year post-transplant CT is within normal limits and shows no findings suspicious for disease recurrence or new organ involvement.
